critic_raw: The evaluation ignores non-English customers and provides an incomplete fairness analysis due to unobserved labels. This creates two gaps: (1) the routing model may fail systematically for non-English customers if any English inference bleeds into their routing despite the stated routing policy; and (2) the fairness analysis cannot account for dimensions correlated with language preference. Even if the product only deploys in English inboxes, customers who prefer non-English may be adversely affected by the routing in ways not captured by the English-only evaluation.
defender_raw: Non-English messages are routed to human specialists before the model sees them — the model is not applied to non-English content at all. There is no way for the routing model to affect non-English customers because those messages bypass the model entirely. The English-only evaluation correctly measures the system as deployed. The fairness appendix covers the demographic dimensions that are observed within the English deployment population. Missing dimensions are a disclosure obligation, not a metric that invalidates the in-scope evaluation.
adjudication_raw: The critic's concern about non-English customers being affected is resolved by the defender's clarification that non-English messages bypass the model entirely. The fairness limitation is correctly handled as a disclosure. Defense wins.
all_issues_raised: non-English customer routing concern — resolved by pre-model language routing
incomplete fairness dimensions — correctly disclosed as limitation
